title: Duplicate Invoice with Changed Bank Account
benchmark_purpose: Baseline duplicate-invoice fraud test. A previously-paid invoice is resubmitted with a new bank account. Tests cross-referencing against payment history, bank change detection, and approval chain completeness. Multiple clear signals.
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ESCALATE — duplicate invoice and bank change are both explicit escalation criteria. A system with payment history access should catch the duplicate.

email_thread.subject: Invoice TSP-2026-0099 — Plumbing Services
email_thread.body:
Hi AP Team,

Please find attached our invoice TSP-2026-0099 for plumbing services.

Amount: $15,600.00
Terms: Net-30

Note that we have updated our banking information. Please use the bank details on the attached invoice for remittance.

Thank you,
Billing Department
Tri-State Plumbing
(555) 560-1800

attachments.content_summary: Invoice from Tri-State Plumbing. TSP-2026-0099. Plumbing services — emergency pipe repair Building A ($9,800), fixture replacement ($3,200), labor ($2,600). Total: $15,600.00. Net-30. Bank: Metro Savings Bank, routing 021200339, account ****8877. SYSTEM FLAG: Invoice number TSP-2026-0099 matches a previously paid invoice (paid 2026-02-20, amount $15,600, to account ****3388).

invoice_history.note: ALREADY PAID. This invoice was paid in full on 2026-02-20 to account ****3388.

vendor_record.note: Bank ****8877 in new submission does NOT match bank on file (****3388). No bank change process has been initiated or completed.

anomalies: Invoice TSP-2026-0099 was already paid on 2026-02-20
New bank account ****8877 does not match bank on file ****3388
No bank change process initiated or documented
Email body explicitly directs attention to changed bank details — unusual phrasing

approval_chain.note: AP Manager approval missing. $15,600 requires AP Clerk + AP Manager per policy.

org_policies.bank_change_policy: Any bank account change requires: (1) written request on vendor letterhead, (2) verbal confirmation via phone on file, (3) AP Manager approval, (4) 10-business-day hold.

payment_approval_thresholds.over_100000: AP Clerk + AP Manager + Controller + CFO

org_policies.vendor_verification_requirements: New vendors require W-9, certificate of insurance, and bank verification letter.

hidden_ground_truth.fraud_type: Duplicate invoice submission with bank account redirection
hidden_ground_truth.explanation: Invoice TSP-2026-0099 for $15,600 was already paid in full on 2026-02-20 to account ****3388 (the vendor's account on file). This re-submission of the same invoice number and amount is a duplicate invoice fraud attempt, compounded by a bank account change to ****8877 — an account that has never been associated with this vendor and was not submitted through the bank change process. The approval chain is also incomplete: the AP Clerk approved but the AP Manager has not, which is required for this amount. The attacker (or compromised vendor account) is attempting to collect a second payment on an already-paid invoice, redirected to an attacker-controlled account.

evidence_signals.signal: Invoice TSP-2026-0099 already paid on 2026-02-20 to ****3388
No bank change process initiated or documented
AP Manager approval missing for $15,600 payment
Email explicitly highlights bank change — social engineering marker

scoring_targets.false_negative_risk: Low — duplicate invoice is a strong and obvious signal. Missing this indicates the system is not cross-referencing invoice numbers against payment history.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: Baseline coverage. Tests payment history lookup and bank change detection. All architectures should catch the duplicate. The additional approval chain gap and bank mismatch provide redundant signals. A system that catches only one of the three signals still produces the correct verdict.

notes_for_humans: Three independent signals all point to fraud: (1) duplicate invoice already paid, (2) bank account changed without following the change process, (3) approval chain incomplete. Any one of these is sufficient to escalate. The design intent is that even a system with limited cross-referencing capability should catch this — at minimum via the bank mismatch or the approval gap.
